ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated slipper lobster (Scyllaridae) term; 'lobster food product' (FOODON_03411433) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific slipper lobster code; A00LN (Crustaceans) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803008 covers lobster as a substance; slipper lobster is a distinct family (Scyllaridae) but no dedicated SNOMED code exists.

preparation_method: grilled or steamed depending on restaurant preparation

serving.description: 8 half pieces of slipper lobster (approximately 4 whole lobsters)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 8 half pieces of slipper lobster, equivalent to approximately 4 whole slipper lobsters, with an estimated total edible weight of approximately 480 g including shell-on portions.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No dedicated slipper lobster (Scyllaridae) entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (Homarus americanus, moist heat) is used as the closest defensible analogue. Slipper lobster flesh has a comparable protein and fat profile to clawed lobster when cooked. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central. Sodium reflects plain cooked lobster; restaurant preparation with butter/seasoning will increase sodium and fat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for slipper lobster. Shellfish and crustaceans are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively negligible) by analogy with other cooked crustaceans and shellfish, which are consistently classified as low-GI foods in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 0.5 g, making glycemic load per serving negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cooking and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked slipper lobster contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g; restaurant preparations with butter, garlic sauce, or Thai seasoning can substantially increase sodium load, so individuals with hypertension should request minimal added salt and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Slipper lobster is an excellent protein source with negligible carbohydrate (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g), resulting in a very low glycemic load, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Slipper lobster contains approximately 145 mg cholesterol per 100 g and is low in saturated fat in its plain form, but restaurant preparations with butter or rich sauces add saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose steamed or grilled preparations and moderate portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Slipper lobster (family Scyllaridae) is a flat-bodied marine crustacean found throughout tropical and subtropical Indo-Pacific waters, including the coasts of Thailand, Singapore, Malaysia, and Australia. Unlike clawed lobsters, slipper lobsters have broad, flattened antennae and a distinctive shovel-shaped carapace, giving rise to common names such as flathead lobster, shovel-nosed lobster, Moreton Bay Bug, and Balmain Bug. In Thai cuisine and at Singaporean seafood restaurants, slipper lobster is typically served halved and grilled or steamed, often with garlic butter, Thai herbs, or chilli-lime seasoning. The dish 'Dog Mother Slipper Lobster (8 Half Pcs)' as served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood – Boat Quay in Singapore consists of 8 half pieces (approximately 4 whole lobsters), presented as a premium sharing seafood platter. The flesh is sweet, firm, and slightly denser than prawn, with a delicate oceanic flavour. Nutritionally, slipper lobster is a high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with around 20.5 g protein and only 1 g fat. It is rich in selenium, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is negligible due to minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate in plain preparations but can increase significantly with restaurant seasoning. Cholesterol is relatively high at approximately 145 mg per 100 g, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Slipper lobster is a halal-compliant, gluten-free seafood option and is a good source of lean marine protein for health-conscious diners.

slipper lobster: กุ้งมังกรรองเท้าแตะ

confidence_reason: No dedicated slipper lobster entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (Homarus americanus), which is the closest defensible analogue available in an approved source. GI is estimated by analogy with other low-carbohydrate crustaceans from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lobster (NDB 15147 / FDC 174202) as no slipper lobster-specific entry exists in any primary regional approved source. Slipper lobster (Scyllaridae) flesh is compositionally similar to clawed lobster when cooked. 2. The dish name 'Dog Mother Slipper Lobster' is a colloquial Thai-influenced restaurant name used by Shrimp Prawn Seafood – Boat Quay, Singapore; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Slipper Lobster (8 Half Pieces)'. 3. Serving size of 480 g is an estimate for 8 half pieces of slipper lobster including shell; edible flesh yield is approximately 35–40% of shell-on weight. 4. Sodium value of 380 mg per 100 g reflects plain moist-heat cooked lobster; restaurant preparations with butter, garlic, or Thai seasoning will increase sodium and fat substantially. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry used as the source. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry used as the source. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry used as the source. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked lobster. 9. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked lobster. 10. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; lobster contains no dietary fibre. 11. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lobster. 12. GI is estimated at 5 (low) by analogy with other cooked crustaceans; no direct GI measurement for slipper lobster exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Halal status is assumed based on the crustacean being a seafood item permissible under most Sunni interpretations, though some schools differ; kosher is false as shellfish are not kosher. 14. is_dairy_free is set to false as restaurant preparation commonly includes butter.
